hyperedges.label: KV-cache compression family (post body-extraction)
hyperedges.source_location: cross-paper: KV-cache memory/bandwidth bottleneck addressed via quantization/sketching, lookup, or in-memory analog

hyperedges.label: Spectral-theoretic polynomial-time invariance pipeline
hyperedges.summary: The finite group G action + Laplace-Beltrami eigen-decomposition + two geometric oracles together yield Theorem 1's poly(n,d,log|G|) exact-invariant estimator.

hyperedges.label: KAT -> learnable edges -> basis parameterization -> trainability
hyperedges.summary: KAT motivates the edge-wise 1D decomposition; smooth (B-spline etc) bases parameterize each edge; smoothness provides trainability; together yield the claimed KAN advantages (interpretability, parameter efficiency).

hyperedges.label: TTT-Discover = entropic objective + PUCT reuse + test-time gradient steps
hyperedges.summary: The discovery-vs-inference distinction motivates both the entropic (max-seeking) objective and PUCT (max-child) reuse; those two plug into the TTT gradient loop, evaluated across four continuous-reward discovery domains.

hyperedges.label: VQC training: angle encoding + PQC + parameter shift + hybrid loop
hyperedges.summary: Angle-rotation encoding feeds the PQC ansatz; parameter-shift rule supplies gradients; classical optimizer closes the hybrid loop; barren plateaus and NP-hardness constrain what counts as a genuine quantum advantage.

All these works share a common research agenda: integrating post-CMOS materials (organic BHJs, memristors, phase-change memory, NS-RAM cells) with CMOS to realize on-chip synaptic plasticity. Charge-trapping in the imager, BiCaLL + BEOL memristor interface in TEXEL, and the memristor / PCM / NS-RAM cells in the Synaptic and Neural Behaviours paper are alternative physical substrates for the same computational abstraction (STP/LTP, STDP, SRDP).

Cluster of methods that reason about the geometry of learned latent/density manifolds: SIGReg shapes embeddings toward an isotropic Gaussian; the OOD paradox paper uses LID estimated via score-rank to detect manifolds of wrong dimension; PPCA assumes Gaussian latents on a linear subspace; State-Region Dictionaries diagnose via Jacobian conditioning. All rely on intrinsic-dimension / Jacobian-rank arguments to characterize what a network has implicitly learned.

nodes.label: Stage-2 Architecture — EGGROLL-PPCA (Perturbation-Pair Crossbar Array)
nodes.summary: Closes Stage-2 learning-rule question from formulation v1 by committing to score-function/ES (EGGROLL) realized as a dual-crossbar (slow G + fast rank-1 ab^T) analog architecture with Gilbert-cell consolidation and subthreshold fitness. Forward-only, all-analog critical path; natural batch size K_batch = τ_slow/τ_fast falls out of device physics. Structural hub unifying five communities.
nodes.norm_label: stage-2 architecture — eggroll-ppca (perturbation-pair crossbar array)

nodes.label: Phase 1 — Perturbation Draw (counter-RNG a,b ~ N(0,σ²))
nodes.summary: Counter-based deterministic RNG produces reproducible a ∈ ℝ^m and b ∈ ℝ^n; written as analog voltages onto the perturbation crossbar rows/columns. Regeneratable at consolidation without storage.
nodes.norm_label: phase 1 — perturbation draw (counter-rng a,b ~ n(0,σ2))

nodes.label: Phase 2 — Combined Forward Pass (y = Gx + α·a·(b·x))
nodes.summary: Input x broadcast to both crossbars. Base crossbar emits Gx via Kirchhoff; perturbation crossbar emits a·(b·x) as a rank-1 structured product. Output summed at current node. One crossbar latency + one rank-1 broadcast — not two MVMs.
nodes.norm_label: phase 2 — combined forward pass (y = gx + α·a·(b·x))

nodes.label: Phase 3 — Nonlinearity N_α via Kink-Biased MOSFETs
nodes.summary: Column of kink-biased MOSFETs implements piecewise-linear N_kink activation (formulation v1 Option α). Activation function is fixed by kink/punch-through I–V device physics, not software.
nodes.norm_label: phase 3 — nonlinearity n_α via kink-biased mosfets

nodes.label: Phase 4 — Inter-Layer Analog Routing (no intermediate digitization)
nodes.summary: Intermediate layer outputs z stay analog and feed the next PPCA layer's x directly. D comparator is only invoked at final output — minimizes ADC overhead. Relies on shared-silicon claim: final-layer MOSFETs dual-role as N+D, earlier layers only N.
nodes.norm_label: phase 4 — inter-layer analog routing (no intermediate digitization)

nodes.label: Phase 5 — Fitness Scoring S = exp(−||z−t||²/2σ²) (single subthreshold transistor)
nodes.summary: Gaussian-approximate EGGROLL score realized in a single subthreshold-exponential transistor; outputs scalar S as a current level on a reference wire.
nodes.norm_label: phase 5 — fitness scoring s = exp(−||z−t||2/2σ2) (single subthreshold transistor)

nodes.label: Phase 6 — Consolidation ΔG_ij = η·S·a_i·b_j via Gilbert-Cell Strip
nodes.summary: Strip of Gilbert cells evaluates four-quadrant S·a_i·b_j; currents program charge-trap cells of the base crossbar. Slow (μs) programming acts as analog low-pass filter over many fast Phase 1–5 iterations; no digital accumulator needed.
nodes.norm_label: phase 6 — consolidation δg_ij = η·s·a_i·b_j via gilbert-cell strip

nodes.label: Lever 1 — Tiling (N×N → (N/k)² tiles)
nodes.summary: Logical N×N crossbar implemented as (N/k)² physical tiles (k≈256). Independent during Phase 2 (no horizontal dependency in Kirchhoff summation). Tile pipelining. Latency O(log(N/k)), area O(N²).
nodes.norm_label: lever 1 — tiling (n×n → (n/k)2 tiles)

nodes.label: Lever 2 — Rank-r Perturbation with Precision-for-Rank Tradeoff
nodes.summary: EGGROLL perturbation is rank-r ≪ min(m,n). Write bandwidth O(r(m+n)) not O(mn). Pair-3 identity r ≤ b·redundancy links cell bit-depth to usable rank: a hardware design knob, not an algorithmic one.
nodes.norm_label: lever 2 — rank-r perturbation with precision-for-rank tradeoff

nodes.label: Lever 3 — Two-Timescale Pipelining (τ_slow ≈ 10 μs, τ_fast ≈ 100 ns)
nodes.summary: Charge-trap programming τ_slow ≈ 10 μs; floating-bulk perturbation and forward/fitness ≈ 100 ns. Natural pipelining of fast inner loop against slow consolidation.
nodes.norm_label: lever 3 — two-timescale pipelining (τ_slow ≈ 10 μs, τ_fast ≈ 100 ns)

nodes.label: Timescale Identity K_batch = τ_slow / (τ_fast_perturb + τ_forward + τ_fitness) ≈ 50
nodes.summary: The natural number of perturbations per consolidation is derived from device timescales, not chosen. ≈ 10 μs / 200 ns ≈ 50, which matches typical EGGROLL K. Falls out of physics; ties device property to algorithmic hyperparameter.
nodes.norm_label: timescale identity k_batch = τ_slow / (τ_fast_perturb + τ_forward + τ_fitness) ≈ 50

nodes.label: Stage-2 Commitment to Score-Function / ES (EGGROLL)
nodes.summary: Of formulation v1's three Stage-2 options, ES is chosen because its five required operations (perturbation draw, forward pass, fitness, outer-product update, consolidation) all already have device-native realizations in the graph. The other two options add teacher wiring or a backward path.
nodes.norm_label: stage-2 commitment to score-function / es (eggroll)

nodes.label: Rejection — Layer-Local (NoProp / Hebbian + Global Signal)
nodes.summary: Layer-local Stage-2 option rejected: requires per-layer teacher wiring, breaks forward-only purity, adds per-layer area cost. Documented as explicit graph-level incompatibility so future additions do not silently reopen Stage-2.
nodes.norm_label: rejection — layer-local (noprop / hebbian + global signal)

nodes.label: Rejection — Surrogate / Straight-Through Estimator
nodes.summary: Surrogate-gradient Stage-2 option rejected: requires an explicit backward path and ~2× silicon area; violates forward-only all-analog critical-path requirement.
nodes.norm_label: rejection — surrogate / straight-through estimator

nodes.label: PPCA Structural Hub — Five-Community God-Node Candidate
nodes.summary: The architecture's neighbors span five distinct communities: Analog Multiplication Family (W, Gilbert), Subthreshold Nonlinearity (N, fitness), Two-Timescale Bridge (charge-trap, floating-bulk), Bit-Level Physics (encoder), Local/Non-Backprop Learning (ES). High betweenness by construction.
nodes.norm_label: ppca structural hub — five-community god-node candidate

nodes.label: Dual-Crossbar Topology — Base G (charge-trap) + Perturbation a·b^T (floating-bulk)
nodes.summary: Two co-located crossbars: the slow parameter G uses charge-trap long-term retention; the fast rank-1 perturbation α·ab^T uses floating-bulk short-term plasticity. Gilbert-cell strip at bottom consolidates.
nodes.norm_label: dual-crossbar topology — base g (charge-trap) + perturbation a·b^t (floating-bulk)

nodes.label: Shared-Silicon Dual Role — Final-Layer MOSFETs Serve N and D
nodes.summary: The D comparator array reuses the same MOSFET species at different bias. Only the final layer's MOSFETs serve dual-role as N+D; earlier layers' MOSFETs serve only as N. Instantiates formulation v1's shared_silicon_claim.
nodes.norm_label: shared-silicon dual role — final-layer mosfets serve n and d

nodes.label: Forward-Only All-Analog Critical Path
nodes.summary: No backward pass, no digitization between layers, no digital accumulator for consolidation. The entire critical path from x to ΔG is analog; only the final D comparator and optional output read are digital.
nodes.norm_label: forward-only all-analog critical path

nodes.label: Analog Low-Pass Consolidation on Charge-Trap
nodes.summary: G(t+T_slow) = G(t) + η·Σ_k S_k · a_k · b_k^T. The floating-gate trap integrates successive subthreshold programming currents; no explicit digital accumulator. Charge-trap slowness IS the low-pass filter.
nodes.norm_label: analog low-pass consolidation on charge-trap

nodes.label: Stage-3 Hints — Open Problems Leaving PPCA
nodes.summary: Three open problems: (1) Gaussian-score learnability under charge-trap noise at O(r⁻¹); (2) cross-tile perturbation correlation biasing the gradient estimator; (3) multi-task/continual learning hierarchy from the two-timescale math.
nodes.norm_label: stage-3 hints — open problems leaving ppca

nodes.label: Rank-1 Perturbation Silicon Realization
nodes.summary: Phase 2's a·(b·x) as one column-sum inner product broadcast across a is the silicon realization of EGGROLL's rank-1 perturbation trick — one crossbar latency plus a broadcast, not two MVMs.
nodes.norm_label: rank-1 perturbation silicon realization

nodes.label: Natural Operating Point at Theoretical O(r⁻¹) Rate
nodes.summary: If EGGROLL's O(r⁻¹) rank-convergence theorem holds, the architecture's physics-derived K_batch ≈ 50 already places it at the theoretical rate — no tuning required.
nodes.norm_label: natural operating point at theoretical o(r−1) rate

nodes.label: Device-Primitive Completeness — Every Op Has a Graph Node
nodes.summary: Every row of the Phase→Operation→Primitive→Graph-Node table has a graph-resident primitive. Nothing in the architecture is invented without prior device grounding.
nodes.norm_label: device-primitive completeness — every op has a graph node

nodes.label: Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.summary: Soleymani, Tahmasebi, Jegelka, Jaillet (2025). First polynomial-time algorithm for exactly G-invariant kernel regression on Riemannian manifolds, achieving the same minimax-optimal excess risk O(n^{-s/(s+d/2)}) as unconstrained KRR while running in poly(n, d, log|G|) instead of Omega(n^2|G|).
nodes.norm_label: learning with exact invariances in polynomial time

nodes.label: Finite group G acting smoothly and isometrically on manifold M
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.summary: Invariance setting is a general finite group G (not just rotations) acting smoothly and isometrically on a compact boundaryless d-dim Riemannian manifold M via theta: G x M -> M. Target regression function f* satisfies f*(gx)=f*(x) for all g in G. Example target: permutation group P_d with |G|=d! which is super-exponential in d and why naive group averaging costs Omega(n^2 |G|).
nodes.norm_label: finite group g acting smoothly and isometrically on manifold m

nodes.label: Theorem 1: poly(n, d, log|G|) algorithm with minimax-optimal risk
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.summary: For f* in Sobolev H^s(M) with s > d/2 and alpha := 2s/d: algorithm runs in O(log^3|G| n^{3/(1+alpha)} + n^{(2+alpha)/(1+alpha)}) time, achieves excess risk R(hat f) = O(n^{-s/(s+d/2)}), requires O(log|G| n^{2/(1+alpha)} + n^{(2+alpha)/(1+alpha)}) oracle calls, and each test-point evaluation costs O(n^{1/(1+alpha)}). First exact-invariance algorithm with polylog dependence on |G|.
nodes.norm_label: theorem 1: poly(n, d, log|g|) algorithm with minimax-optimal risk

nodes.label: Laplace-Beltrami eigenbasis reformulation
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 1 pp.1-2, Section 3 oracles, Section 5 proof sketch
nodes.summary: Key technical move: because the Laplace-Beltrami operator commutes with every isometric G-action on M, one can pick a basis of Laplacian eigenfunctions phi_{lambda,ell} so that each g acts on eigenspaces by orthogonal matrices. The nonconvex invariant-kernel-regression problem decomposes into an infinite collection of finite-dimensional convex quadratic programs with linear constraints (one per eigenspace). Truncating the eigen-cut yields the poly-time estimator.
nodes.norm_label: laplace-beltrami eigenbasis reformulation

nodes.label: Group averaging baseline is intractable for large |G|
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 3, p.3, K_inv definition and complexity discussion
nodes.summary: Naive solutions (data augmentation, group averaging K_inv(x1,x2) = (1/|G|) sum_g K(gx1,x2), canonicalization, frame averaging) either cost Omega(n |G|) or Omega(n^2 |G|), or suffer discontinuities that break RKHS requirements. This motivates needing an exact-invariance algorithm whose runtime is polylogarithmic in |G|.
nodes.norm_label: group averaging baseline is intractable for large |g|

nodes.label: Eigenfunction evaluation and shifted inner-product oracles
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.summary: Algorithm assumes two oracles: (1) point-evaluate any eigenfunction phi_{lambda,ell}(x); (2) compute L2(M) inner products <phi_{lambda,ell}(gx), phi_{lambda,ell'}(x)> for any g in G. Both are free as long as the cumulative eigenfunction count D_lambda is poly(n,d). Natural for spheres S^{d-1} (spherical harmonics), Stiefel manifolds, and tori.
nodes.norm_label: eigenfunction evaluation and shifted inner-product oracles

nodes.label: Polynomial-time / polylogarithmic-in-|G| complexity regime
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.summary: The statistical-computational trade-off achieved sits in P: running time is poly(n, d, log|G|). Enumerating G (which can be super-exponential in d, e.g. d!) is provably unnecessary for statistically efficient exact-invariant estimation under Sobolev priors. This is in contrast to Omega(n^2 |G|) group averaging and super-exponential data augmentation.
nodes.norm_label: polynomial-time / polylogarithmic-in-|g| complexity regime

nodes.label: A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.summary: Noorizadegan, Wang, Ling (2025). Systematic review of KAN architecture: basis function choices (B-splines, Chebyshev, Jacobi, RBF, Fourier, wavelet, sinc), KAT-to-KAN translation, bridging KANs and MLPs, accuracy/efficiency/regularization improvements, a Choose-Your-KAN practical guide.
nodes.norm_label: a practitioner's guide to kolmogorov-arnold networks

nodes.label: Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem (KAT)
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.summary: Every continuous f: [0,1]^n -> R admits f(x_1,...,x_n) = sum_{q=0}^{2n} Phi_q ( sum_{p=1}^n phi_{q,p}(x_p) ) with continuous univariate inner maps phi_{q,p}: [0,1] -> R and outer maps Phi_q: R -> R. Inner maps can be chosen universal (independent of f); outer maps carry f-dependence. Lorentz and Sprecher refinements give simpler forms (single outer Phi, single inner phi with shifts eta_q and offsets c_q).
nodes.norm_label: kolmogorov-arnold representation theorem (kat)

nodes.label: Learnable univariate activations on edges (vs fixed MLP node activations)
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 3 'From KAT to KAN' Eq. (6), Section 4 'Bridging KANs and MLPs'
nodes.summary: KAN layer: x_q^{(l+1)} = sum_{p=1}^P phi_{q,p}^{(l)}( x_p^{(l)} ). Each edge (p,q) carries its own learnable 1D nonlinearity; no linear mix before activation. MLP is mix-then-activate with fixed sigma; KAN is activate-then-sum. This reversal yields parameter efficiency: KAN depth L width W grid G costs O(G W^2 L) vs equivalent MLP's O(G^2 W^4 L).
nodes.norm_label: learnable univariate activations on edges (vs fixed mlp node activations)

nodes.label: B-spline basis parameterization per edge
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.summary: Standard KAN parameterizes each edge as phi_{q,p}^{(l)}(x) = sum_{k=1}^K c_{q,p,k}^{(l)} B_k(x) with learnable coefficients c_{q,p,k} and fixed B-spline basis {B_k}. Spline locality+smoothness gives compact, interpretable per-edge 1D transforms. Other bases (Chebyshev, Jacobi, Gaussian RBF, Fourier, ReLU^k, wavelet, sinc) can be substituted without changing (6).
nodes.norm_label: b-spline basis parameterization per edge

nodes.label: Trainability via smooth differentiable basis (modernizes pathological KAT)
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 3 'Remark' p.6, and Section 3 'Note that the exact inner functions constructed in KAT are often pathological'
nodes.summary: Classical KAT constructions used highly irregular (fractal-like, non-Lipschitz) inner functions, which made the depth-2 width-(2n+1) realization hard to optimize. Modern KANs dodge this by replacing exact KAT maps with smooth learnable parameterizations (splines / polynomials / RBFs), enabling end-to-end gradient training.
nodes.norm_label: trainability via smooth differentiable basis (modernizes pathological kat)

nodes.label: Claimed advantages: interpretability, parameter efficiency, sample/convergence gains
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 4 'Bridging KANs and MLPs' summary p.7, Table 2 pp.3-4
nodes.summary: KAN literature claims: (i) interpretability via inspectable per-edge 1D maps; (ii) parameter count O(GW^2L) vs MLP O(G^2W^4L); (iii) often faster convergence on PDE / regression tasks despite slower per-iteration cost; (iv) better accuracy for symbolic and physics-informed regimes when basis matches problem structure. Authors caution gains depend strongly on basis choice and are not monolithic.
nodes.norm_label: claimed advantages: interpretability, parameter efficiency, sample/convergence gains

nodes.label: Learning to Discover at Test Time (TTT-Discover)
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning to Discover at Test Time.pdf
nodes.source_location: whole paper; Yuksekgonul, Koceja, Li, Bianchi, Sun et al. (Stanford/NVIDIA/UCSD)
nodes.summary: Performs reinforcement learning at test time on a single test problem using gpt-oss-120b + LoRA r=32 via Tinker, 50 steps x 512 rollouts. Sets SOTA on Erdos' min-overlap, autocorrelation inequality, GPUMode TriMul kernel competition (up to 2x faster), AtCoder algorithms, single-cell denoising.
nodes.norm_label: learning to discover at test time (ttt-discover)

nodes.label: Test-time training: gradient updates to LLM weights while solving one problem
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning to Discover at Test Time.pdf
nodes.summary: Update rule: theta_{i+1} = theta_i + eta * grad_theta J_beta(s_i)(theta_i) with reuse s_i ~ PUCT(H_i). Iteratively samples rollouts a_i ~ pi_{theta_i}(.|d,s_i,c_i), transitions to s'_i, rewards r_i, appends to buffer H, then takes one gradient step per batch of 512. Uses LoRA rank 32 on gpt-oss-120b, importance-sampling correction for sampler/learner mismatch.
nodes.norm_label: test-time training: gradient updates to llm weights while solving one problem

nodes.label: Entropic objective J_beta favoring max-reward actions
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning to Discover at Test Time.pdf
nodes.summary: J_beta(theta) = E_{s~reuse(H)} [ log E_{a~pi_theta(.|s)} [ e^{beta(s) R(s,a)} ] ]. Gradient weights w_{beta(s)}(a) = e^{beta R} / E[e^{beta R}]. As beta -> infty objective -> max instead of mean. beta(s) is set adaptively per initial state by KL-constraining induced policy, avoiding instabilities at large beta and vanishing advantages at small beta. This is the signal that guides test-time adaptation: max-seeking, not mean-seeking.
nodes.norm_label: entropic objective j_beta favoring max-reward actions

nodes.label: Discovery vs standard-RL-inference distinction
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning to Discover at Test Time.pdf
nodes.summary: Standard RL finds a policy maximizing expected reward for repeated deployment; policy is the product. Discovery aims for ONE state s with R(s) > r_sota on this very problem; the policy is only a means, no deployment phase exists, and a policy can have low mean reward so long as it achieves SOTA once. This asymmetry motivates (i) the entropic max-seeking objective, (ii) PUCT with max (not mean) of child rewards for Q(s), (iii) solution reuse to extend effective horizon.
nodes.norm_label: discovery vs standard-rl-inference distinction

nodes.label: PUCT-inspired reuse for initial-state selection
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning to Discover at Test Time.pdf
nodes.summary: Initial-state selection scores s by Q(s) + c P(s) sqrt(1+T)/(1+n(s)), where Q(s) = MAX reward of children (not mean), P(s) is reward-rank prior, n(s) visit count, T total expansions. Captures 'fast kernel seeds faster kernel' intuition while exploration bonus prevents over-exploitation. Extends effective horizon via state reuse since discovery has no fixed initial-state distribution.
nodes.norm_label: puct-inspired reuse for initial-state selection

nodes.label: Continuous-reward discovery domains: math, GPU kernels, algorithms, biology
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning to Discover at Test Time.pdf
nodes.summary: TTT-Discover restricted to continuous-reward problems. Applications: (1) Erdos minimum-overlap problem (reward = 1/upper_bound); (2) autocorrelation inequalities; (3) GPU kernel engineering (GPUMode TriMul, reward = 1/runtime; 2198us on A100, 1161us on H100 beating human 1371us); (4) AtCoder Heuristic Contest 39 (test score); (5) single-cell denoising (1/MSE, 0.71 vs human 0.64).
nodes.norm_label: continuous-reward discovery domains: math, gpu kernels, algorithms, biology

nodes.label: A Primer on Quantum Machine Learning
nodes.source_file: raw/A Primer on Quantum Machine Learning.pdf
nodes.summary: High-level review of QML focusing on settings where the quantum device is the primary learning unit. Covers variational QML, data embeddings, quantum kernels, QNNs, PAC learning separations, barren plateaus, dequantization, and honest accounting of where quantum advantages are real vs conditional vs contrived.
nodes.norm_label: a primer on quantum machine learning

nodes.label: Variational quantum circuit (PQC / QNN)
nodes.source_file: raw/A Primer on Quantum Machine Learning.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section II.C 'Parametrized quantum circuits and quantum neural networks', p.11; Eq. (7) p.7
nodes.summary: PQC ansatz U(theta) = product of e^{-i theta_l H_l} over gate generators H_l in G = {H_1,...,H_p}. Output: y~_theta(x) = Tr[rho(x,theta) O] where rho(x,theta) is data-encoded state and O is task observable. 'QNN' and 'PQC' are used interchangeably, though unlike classical NNs there is no native nonlinearity or fan-out (no-cloning).
nodes.norm_label: variational quantum circuit (pqc / qnn)

nodes.label: Angle-rotation data encoding and theta of rotation gates
nodes.source_file: raw/A Primer on Quantum Machine Learning.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section II.A 'Embedding classical data' pp.7-8; Fig 4(a)
nodes.summary: Features x_j encoded as rotation angles: R_{v_j}(x_j) = e^{-i x_j v_j . sigma / 2} on qubit j, e.g. V_v(x) = tensor_j e^{-i nu_j x_j Y_j}. Trainable gates use angles theta_l similarly: e^{-i theta_l . sigma / 2}. The same angle parameterization serves both data embedding and trainable rotations -- 'theta is the knob.' Care needed: embedding introduces periodicity, scale sensitivity, and bandwidth-dependent inductive bias.
nodes.norm_label: angle-rotation data encoding and theta of rotation gates

nodes.label: Parameter-shift rule for VQC gradients
nodes.source_file: raw/A Primer on Quantum Machine Learning.pdf
nodes.summary: To compute d y~_theta(x) / d theta_l, the parameter-shift rule evaluates the SAME circuit used for y~_theta(x) at two angle-shifted points (typically theta_l +- pi/2) and takes a linear combination. Works for generators with two distinct eigenvalues. Cost: one gradient entry per pair of shifted-circuit measurements, so full gradient over T parameters costs O(T) circuit evaluations; genuine quantum backprop is ruled out by quantum constraints (no-cloning, measurement collapse).
nodes.norm_label: parameter-shift rule for vqc gradients

nodes.label: Hybrid classical-quantum training loop
nodes.source_file: raw/A Primer on Quantum Machine Learning.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section II introduction and II.C, pp.7, 11; Fig. 3
nodes.summary: Pipeline: (1) quantum device prepares rho(x,theta) via angle-encoding + parameterized unitary; (2) measures observable O to produce finite-shot estimate y~_theta(x); (3) classical computer assembles empirical loss hat R_S and chooses next theta via classical optimizer (often using parameter-shift gradients). This outsources heavy optimization to classical side, lowering gate/ancilla burden -- attractive for NISQ hardware.
nodes.norm_label: hybrid classical-quantum training loop

nodes.label: Quantum advantage: provable separations vs dequantized heuristics
nodes.source_file: raw/A Primer on Quantum Machine Learning.pdf
nodes.source_location: Abstract, Section III 'Quantum computational learning theory and provable separations'
nodes.summary: What counts as quantum advantage is nuanced. For quantum data: measured improvements in experimental information extraction. For classical data: heuristic gains coexist with no-go theorems; known separations exist for contrived concept classes (Quantum PAC with quantum-example oracles); other once-hopeful advantages have been dequantized. Barren plateaus and local-minima issues plague variational QML; many barren-plateau mitigations themselves dequantize the model. VQC trainability vs classical simulability is in tension.
nodes.norm_label: quantum advantage: provable separations vs dequantized heuristics

nodes.label: Barren plateaus and NP-hard VQC training
nodes.source_file: raw/A Primer on Quantum Machine Learning.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section II.D 'Challenges and limitations for variational QML' p.11-12
nodes.summary: Loss landscape differences (y~_{theta1}(x) - y~_{theta2}(x)) can vanish as O(1/dim(H)) on average or for all pairs, requiring exponentially many shots with N_s ~ 1/sqrt(N_s) error to distinguish. This curse-of-dimensionality precludes large-scale training. Mitigation techniques that keep trainability typically make the model classically simulable, erasing quantum advantage. Training VQCs is provably NP-hard (Bittel & Kliesch 2021).
nodes.norm_label: barren plateaus and np-hard vqc training

nodes.label: Why KAN edge-functions and exact invariances are disjoint topics
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.source_location: KAN paper: grep for 'invariance'/'equivariance'/'group' returns only Section 5 SPEL-KAN Lie-symmetry extension mention; Invariances paper grep for 'Kolmogorov'/'KAN' returns zero hits
nodes.summary: Body-read resolves the prior AMBIGUOUS KAN<->Invariances edge. The KAN survey is about 1D basis parameterizations on edges (B-splines, Chebyshev, Fourier) of a feedforward decomposition f = sum_q Phi_q(sum_p phi_{qp}(x_p)); it does not address group-invariant function classes (only one passing reference to SPEL-KAN with Lie symmetries). The Invariances paper is about kernel regression on Riemannian manifolds under finite group G-actions via Laplace-Beltrami eigen-decomposition; it never references Kolmogorov-Arnold or KANs. The two methods live on different axes: architecture parameterization vs statistical-computational trade-offs of symmetry.
nodes.norm_label: why kan edge-functions and exact invariances are disjoint topics

nodes.label: CMOS-integrated Organic Neuromorphic Imagers for Dual-Modal Imaging
nodes.summary: A 640x512-pixel CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imager that operates in two modes: standard frame-based imaging and synaptic neuromorphic imaging. The synaptic mode extracts high-resolution spatiotemporal maps (light distribution and motion trajectories) from final frames by decoding temporal sequences of light events through contrast analysis. Adjustable memory (delay 10^1 to 10^4 ms) via modulation of charge recombination-trapping dynamics; retention >18 min. Demonstrates potential for industrial monitoring and motion detection.
nodes.source_file: raw/CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for high-resolution dual-modal imaging.pdf
nodes.norm_label: cmos-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for dual-modal imaging

nodes.label: Hybrid CMOS + organic BHJ device stack
nodes.summary: Lateral two-terminal organic neuromorphic device with donor-acceptor bulk heterojunctions (BHJ) of PTB7-Th and PZ1 between Au electrodes, with (3-Mercaptopropyl)trimethoxysilane (MPTS) as an interfacial trap layer. Without MPTS, memory effects are negligible. The BHJ + trap stack is co-integrated with a readout integrated circuit (ROIC) via a CMOS-compatible photolithography method using photocrosslinking polymers, enabling non-destructive patterning down to ~1 um and monolithic integration of organic synaptic pixels on CMOS.
nodes.source_file: raw/CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for high-resolution dual-modal imaging.pdf
nodes.norm_label: hybrid cmos + organic bhj device stack

nodes.label: Dual-modal operation: static frame + dynamic synaptic
nodes.summary: The imager operates in two modes. Standard mode: conventional frame-based static image capture via the ROIC. Synaptic mode: the organic device's photonic non-volatility (STP->LTP transition, paired-pulse facilitation, retention >18 min) accumulates light events so that a single final frame encodes the motion trajectory / light-event distribution map over tens of minutes. Dynamic information is preprocessed inside the pixel, eliminating the need to store/transmit every frame.
nodes.source_file: raw/CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for high-resolution dual-modal imaging.pdf
nodes.norm_label: dual-modal operation: static frame + dynamic synaptic

nodes.label: Charge recombination-trapping dynamics as tunable memory mechanism
nodes.summary: The learning/memory mechanism is physical rather than algorithmic: photogenerated electrons trapped at the MPTS interface layer while holes flow, leading to a sublinear EPSC rise/decay. The decay time constant tau_0 (time to 1/e of peak EPSC) is tuned by the weight percentage of PZ1, which modifies the density of recombination centers. This gives adjustable delay from 10^1 to 10^4 ms, STP->LTP transitions, photonic writing and electrical erasing at -5 V, and energy of ~4 fJ per event at 0.2 V / 5 ms.
nodes.source_file: raw/CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for high-resolution dual-modal imaging.pdf
nodes.norm_label: charge recombination-trapping dynamics as tunable memory mechanism

nodes.label: CMOS-compatible photocrosslinking patterning for organic BHJs
nodes.summary: A photolithography method that uses photocrosslinking between polymers to pattern organic bulk heterojunctions non-destructively at ~1 um resolution, the key enabling step for integrating organic neuromorphic devices with wafer-scale CMOS readout at 640x512 resolution. Prior organic neuromorphic devices could not be patterned without damage, which had previously blocked high-resolution dual-modal imagers.
nodes.source_file: raw/CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for high-resolution dual-modal imaging.pdf
nodes.norm_label: cmos-compatible photocrosslinking patterning for organic bhjs

nodes.label: LeWorldModel: Stable End-to-End JEPA from Pixels
nodes.summary: LeWorldModel (LeWM) is the first Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) that trains stably end-to-end from raw pixels using only two loss terms: a next-embedding prediction loss and SIGReg (a Sketched-Isotropic-Gaussian regularizer) enforcing Gaussian-distributed latents. 15M parameters trainable on a single GPU in hours; plans up to 48x faster than foundation-model world models like DINO-WM while being competitive across 2D and 3D control tasks. Reduces loss hyperparameters from six to one.
nodes.norm_label: leworldmodel: stable end-to-end jepa from pixels

nodes.label: Latent-space next-embedding prediction (JEPA, no pixel reconstruction)
nodes.summary: LeWM predicts the embedding z_{t+1} of the next frame, not the pixels. An encoder (ViT-Tiny, ~5M params, patch 14, [CLS] token + BN MLP projector) maps frames o_t into compact latents z_t. A 6-layer, 16-head transformer predictor takes (z_t, a_t) via Adaptive LayerNorm (AdaLN, zero-init) and auto-regressively predicts z_{t+1}. Training objective is MSE between predicted z_hat_{t+1} and target z_{t+1} (teacher forcing). Reward-free, reconstruction-free, task-agnostic.
nodes.norm_label: latent-space next-embedding prediction (jepa, no pixel reconstruction)

nodes.label: SIGReg anti-collapse regularizer
nodes.summary: To prevent trivial representation collapse from the prediction loss, LeWM uses the Sketched-Isotropic-Gaussian Regularizer (SIGReg). Latent embeddings are projected onto many random univariate directions and a normality test is applied to each projection; aggregated statistics push the full embedding distribution toward an isotropic Gaussian. Provides provable anti-collapse guarantees, replacing EMA target encoders, stop-gradients, pre-trained encoders, and VICReg-style multi-term losses used by prior JEPAs (I-JEPA, V-JEPA, DINO-WM, PLDM).
nodes.norm_label: sigreg anti-collapse regularizer

nodes.label: Model-predictive control in latent space
nodes.summary: At test time LeWM plans by MPC: encode initial obs o_1 and goal o_g into z_1 and z_g, then autoregressively roll out future latents with the predictor over horizon H; a latent cost between the final predicted state and z_g guides a solver to optimize the action sequence; predict-optimize loop iterated until convergence. With ~200x fewer tokens than DINO-WM, planning is ~47x faster (0.98 s vs 47 s averaged over 50 runs).
nodes.norm_label: model-predictive control in latent space

nodes.label: Emergent physical structure in latent space
nodes.summary: Beyond control, LeWM's latent space encodes meaningful physical structure: linear probes on z recover physical quantities (positions, velocities, etc.), and a violation-of-expectation / 'surprise' evaluation reliably detects physically implausible trajectories. This supports JEPA's premise that predictable latents implicitly capture physics without pixel reconstruction or reward supervision.
nodes.norm_label: emergent physical structure in latent space

nodes.label: Geometric Explanation of the Likelihood OOD Detection Paradox
nodes.summary: Kamkari et al. (ICML 2024) explain why likelihood-based deep generative models (normalizing flows, diffusion) assign higher likelihoods to OOD samples from simpler datasets yet never generate them. Core claim: high-density regions can contain minimal probability mass when data concentrates on lower-dimensional manifolds. The paradox is resolvable by pairing model likelihood with local intrinsic dimension (LID) estimated from the pre-trained DGM; the resulting detector matches/surpasses SOTA using the same DGM backbone.
nodes.source_file: raw/A Geometric Explanation of the Likelihood OOD Detection Paradox.pdf
nodes.norm_label: geometric explanation of the likelihood ood detection paradox

nodes.label: Manifold hypothesis and the density-vs-mass distinction
nodes.summary: Natural data lies on low-dimensional submanifolds of R^d. A density p_theta may be highly peaked around an OOD manifold of lower dimension than the training manifold, yielding high likelihood yet near-zero probability mass because the manifold has negligible d-dimensional volume. Example: FMNIST-trained DGM peaked around a 1D MNIST manifold in 2D shows high density but vanishing mass. This separates 'where the model puts density' from 'what the model actually samples', reconciling the two-pronged paradox.
nodes.source_file: raw/A Geometric Explanation of the Likelihood OOD Detection Paradox.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 1 + Fig. 1 + Section 2 Local Intrinsic Dimension
nodes.norm_label: manifold hypothesis and the density-vs-mass distinction

nodes.label: LID-via-score-rank estimator (Jacobian/score quantity)
nodes.summary: The geometric quantity that separates ID from OOD is the local intrinsic dimension LID_theta(x) of the model-implied manifold M_theta at x. Following Stanczuk et al., for a variance-exploding score-based DM one runs k independent noised trajectories from x up to small t_0 and evaluates the score s_theta(x_i, t_0); these lie in the normal space to M_theta at x. The matrix S(x)=[s_theta(x_1,t_0)|...|s_theta(x_k,t_0)] in R^{d x k} has rank equal to the normal-space dimension, and LID_theta(x) is approx d - rank S(x). For normalizing flows, the log-det Jacobian of f_theta plays the analogous geometric role.
nodes.source_file: raw/A Geometric Explanation of the Likelihood OOD Detection Paradox.pdf
nodes.norm_label: lid-via-score-rank estimator (jacobian/score quantity)

nodes.label: Dual-criterion OOD detector: high likelihood AND high LID
nodes.summary: A datum is classified in-distribution only if it (i) has high model likelihood p_theta(x) and (ii) lies in a region with non-negligible probability mass, operationalized as large LID_theta(x) estimated from a single pre-trained DGM. Applied to normalizing flows and variance-exploding DMs, it matches or beats SOTA OOD-detection benchmarks (FMNIST vs MNIST/Omniglot, CIFAR10/CelebA vs SVHN) while using the same DGM backbone as baselines.
nodes.source_file: raw/A Geometric Explanation of the Likelihood OOD Detection Paradox.pdf
nodes.norm_label: dual-criterion ood detector: high likelihood and high lid

nodes.label: TEXEL: Mixed-Signal Neuromorphic Processor for Beyond-CMOS Integration
nodes.summary: TEXEL is a fabricated mixed-signal neuromorphic SNN processor (Greatorex et al., Nat. Commun. 2025) that combines subthreshold-CMOS spiking neurons and analog plastic synapses with a Back-End-of-Line (BEOL) device-agnostic differential synaptic interface for ~10K plastic synapses. The interface supports a wide range of two- and three-terminal memristive devices atop the CMOS chip, serving as a platform to test bio-inspired on-chip learning alongside emerging beyond-CMOS devices. Network-level experiments run Vector-Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) on-chip.
nodes.source_file: raw/A neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning for beyond-CMOS device integration.pdf
nodes.norm_label: texel: mixed-signal neuromorphic processor for beyond-cmos integration

nodes.label: BiCaLL on-chip local learning rule (STDP + Hebbian/SRDP)
nodes.summary: On-chip plasticity is implemented with mixed-signal circuitry embedded in each plastic synapse that emulates the Bistable Calcium-based Local Learning (BiCaLL) rule: STDP dominates at low activity (good for temporal pattern recognition with sparse spikes), Hebbian/Spike-Rate-Dependent Plasticity (SRDP) dominates at high activity (good for rate-based learning and evidence accumulation). A slower postsynaptic Ca2+ trace acts as a plasticity-gating signal that restricts updates to specific firing-rate ranges.
nodes.source_file: raw/A neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning for beyond-CMOS device integration.pdf
nodes.norm_label: bicall on-chip local learning rule (stdp + hebbian/srdp)

nodes.label: Bistable binary plastic synapse with analog internal weight
nodes.summary: Each plastic synapse quantizes to a binary efficacy but maintains a bistable analog internal weight V_w. Bistability circuitry pushes V_w toward one of two attractor values after accumulated updates, mitigating catastrophic forgetting in binary synapses. The on-chip weight matrix can be read out after learning and programmed for inference. This addresses the classical trade-off in pure-CMOS neuromorphic systems between silicon-area cost of storing analog weights and stability of long-term memory.
nodes.source_file: raw/A neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning for beyond-CMOS device integration.pdf
nodes.norm_label: bistable binary plastic synapse with analog internal weight

nodes.label: BEOL differential interface for two- and three-terminal memristors
nodes.summary: Device class: the chip is not wedded to one memristor type; its BEOL differential synaptic interface supports two- and three-terminal memristive devices (e.g. Valence Change Mechanism (VCM) cells are simulated, plus phase-change and ferroelectric candidates from collaborating groups) integrated atop the CMOS. The differential interface enables high-frequency, temporally unstructured reprogramming (needed for real-time on-chip learning) while retaining weight-read functionality. Scale: ~10K plastic synapses in a single chip, an order-of-magnitude step beyond prior test-structure demonstrations.
nodes.source_file: raw/A neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning for beyond-CMOS device integration.pdf
nodes.norm_label: beol differential interface for two- and three-terminal memristors

nodes.label: Subthreshold analog + event-driven asynchronous architecture
nodes.summary: TEXEL exploits the subthreshold-CMOS regime for neuron and synapse dynamics combined with asynchronous event-based digital routing (spikes are digital events). This yields ultra-low-power SNN operation suited to edge applications like bio-signal and environmental monitoring. Power measurements per circuit block quantify the efficiency gains from subthreshold analog + event-driven signaling, establishing a baseline for future fully-memristive variants.
nodes.source_file: raw/A neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning for beyond-CMOS device integration.pdf
nodes.norm_label: subthreshold analog + event-driven asynchronous architecture

nodes.label: Rationale: LeWM and TTT-Discover target different paradigms
nodes.summary: LeWorldModel and Learning to Discover at Test Time (TTT-Discover) were tagged AMBIGUOUS because both touch 'test-time' and 'prediction'. After reading bodies: LeWM is an offline-trained JEPA world model that, at inference, runs MPC planning in a frozen latent space; no weights are updated at test time. TTT-Discover is RL at test time on an LLM to solve a single hard scientific problem; weights are explicitly trained at test time. They share no architecture (ViT+transformer predictor vs LLM policy), no objective (next-latent MSE + SIGReg vs RL with continuous rewards), no modality (pixels+actions from robotics datasets vs math/kernels/algorithms), and no learning regime (frozen at test time vs updated at test time). They are disjoint.
nodes.norm_label: rationale: lewm and ttt-discover target different paradigms

hyperedges.label: KV-cache compression family (post body-extraction)
hyperedges.source_location: cross-paper: KV-cache memory/bandwidth bottleneck addressed via quantization/sketching, lookup, or in-memory analog

hyperedges.label: Spectral-theoretic polynomial-time invariance pipeline
hyperedges.summary: The finite group G action + Laplace-Beltrami eigen-decomposition + two geometric oracles together yield Theorem 1's poly(n,d,log|G|) exact-invariant estimator.

hyperedges.label: KAT -> learnable edges -> basis parameterization -> trainability
hyperedges.summary: KAT motivates the edge-wise 1D decomposition; smooth (B-spline etc) bases parameterize each edge; smoothness provides trainability; together yield the claimed KAN advantages (interpretability, parameter efficiency).

hyperedges.label: TTT-Discover = entropic objective + PUCT reuse + test-time gradient steps
hyperedges.summary: The discovery-vs-inference distinction motivates both the entropic (max-seeking) objective and PUCT (max-child) reuse; those two plug into the TTT gradient loop, evaluated across four continuous-reward discovery domains.

hyperedges.label: VQC training: angle encoding + PQC + parameter shift + hybrid loop
hyperedges.summary: Angle-rotation encoding feeds the PQC ansatz; parameter-shift rule supplies gradients; classical optimizer closes the hybrid loop; barren plateaus and NP-hardness constrain what counts as a genuine quantum advantage.

All these works share a common research agenda: integrating post-CMOS materials (organic BHJs, memristors, phase-change memory, NS-RAM cells) with CMOS to realize on-chip synaptic plasticity. Charge-trapping in the imager, BiCaLL + BEOL memristor interface in TEXEL, and the memristor / PCM / NS-RAM cells in the Synaptic and Neural Behaviours paper are alternative physical substrates for the same computational abstraction (STP/LTP, STDP, SRDP).

Cluster of methods that reason about the geometry of learned latent/density manifolds: SIGReg shapes embeddings toward an isotropic Gaussian; the OOD paradox paper uses LID estimated via score-rank to detect manifolds of wrong dimension; PPCA assumes Gaussian latents on a linear subspace; State-Region Dictionaries diagnose via Jacobian conditioning. All rely on intrinsic-dimension / Jacobian-rank arguments to characterize what a network has implicitly learned.
